findings.summary: git_list_branches returns status 'success' while every other git MCP tool returns 'ok'
evidence: 34 occurrences of '"status": "ok"' in git mcp_tools.py vs 1 occurrence of '"status": "success"' in git_list_branches (line 187)
git_check_availability and git_list_branches lack try/except blocks while all 33 other functions have them, leaving them unable to return the standard error shape on failure
findings.suggestion: Change git_list_branches to return '"status": "ok"' and wrap the call in try/except returning '{"status": "error", "error": str(exc)}' on failure, matching all sibling tools.

findings.summary: branching.py returns False on failure instead of raising, losing error context at the caller
evidence: create_branch, switch_branch, delete_branch, get_current_branch all catch exceptions and return False or None with only a logger.error call
The mcp_tools.py layer wraps these in try/except expecting exceptions to propagate, but they never do — the MCP tool returns {"status": "ok"} even when the underlying operation silently failed
findings.suggestion: Remove the bare-except-return-False pattern in branching commands. Let CalledProcessError propagate so mcp_tools.py try/except can catch it and return the error shape.

findings.summary: trust_gateway has two separate trust states (_trust_level global and _registry) that can diverge
evidence: Module-level trust_tool() (line 614) calls _registry.trust_tool() but never updates _trust_level
trust_all() (line 635) updates both state variables but trust_tool() only updates one

get_current_trust_level() (line 790) reads _trust_level, so after calling trust_tool('codomyrmex.write_file'), is_trusted() returns True via _registry but get_current_trust_level() still returns UNTRUSTED
findings.suggestion: Remove the redundant _trust_level global variable. Derive the aggregate level from _registry.get_aggregate_level() in get_current_trust_level() to keep a single source of truth.

findings.summary: 28 TypedDict and dataclass definitions use 'Functional component: X.' as the entire docstring, adding no information
evidence: Pattern '"""Functional component: X."""' found 28 times across production code
trust_gateway.py line 87: 'class AuditEntry(TypedDict): """Functional component: AuditEntry."""' — the docstring merely restates the class name
This is a hallmark AI-generation artifact: every instance adds zero informational value beyond what the class name already conveys
findings.suggestion: Replace each 'Functional component: X.' docstring with a description of the type's purpose and fields.

findings.summary: 19.2% of test functions (4014 of 20892) have 'Test functionality: X.' as their entire docstring
evidence: Pattern '"""Test functionality: <name>."""' appears 4014 times in the test suite
test_swarm.py: 'def test_auto_id: """Test functionality: auto id."""' — restates only the test name with zero behavioral context
These docstrings add no information about preconditions, inputs, or expected outcomes — a reader learns nothing beyond what the function name already says
findings.suggestion: Replace with scenario-describing docstrings that state the precondition and expected outcome, e.g. 'SwarmMessage auto-assigns a non-empty message_id on construction.'

findings.summary: scipy, fastapi, uvicorn, pylint, bandit, numpy, and loguru are unconditional core dependencies for all installs
evidence: pyproject.toml [project.dependencies] includes 'scipy>=1.7.0' (used only by cerebrum), 'fastapi>=0.100.0' and 'uvicorn>=0.23.0' (used only by API/server modules)
'pylint>=3.0.0', 'bandit>=1.8.0', 'radon>=6.0.0' are only used by static_analysis module but forced on all installs
'loguru>=0.7.0' is not the project's logging framework — 80+ files use stdlib logging vs 2 files using loguru — yet it is a mandatory core dep
findings.suggestion: Move scipy to a 'cerebrum' optional extra, fastapi/uvicorn to an 'api' extra, pylint/bandit/radon to 'static_analysis' extra, and remove loguru from core deps.

findings.summary: cryptography and msgpack appear in both core dependencies and optional extras at conflicting version constraints
evidence: cryptography: 'cryptography>=41.0.0' in [project.dependencies] AND 'cryptography>=42.0.0' in [project.optional-dependencies.crypto] — different lower bounds
msgpack: 'msgpack>=1.1.2' in [project.dependencies] AND 'msgpack>=1.0.0' in [project.optional-dependencies.serialization] — conflicting lower bounds
Duplicate declarations suggest the optional extras were added without auditing the base list, creating version resolution ambiguity
findings.suggestion: Remove cryptography and msgpack from [project.dependencies] if they are optional-only, or remove them from the optional extras if they truly belong in core.

findings.summary: terminal_interface (Foundation layer) imports system_discovery and coding (Core layer) at constructor time
evidence: interactive_shell.py constructor (__init__) at line 57-60 imports SystemDiscovery from codomyrmex.system_discovery.core.discovery_engine directly inside __init__
The file comment (line 17-18) acknowledges these are 'Core-layer imports', confirming the author was aware of the layering violation
Foundation layer should only import from Foundation layer — this creates an upward dependency in the wrong direction
findings.suggestion: Move SystemDiscovery and coding imports to the methods that use them (lazy import pattern) rather than the constructor, keeping constructor imports at the Foundation layer only.

findings.summary: Legacy SwarmManager.execute() returns hardcoded fake strings 'Result from {agent_id}' with no actual execution
evidence: SwarmManager.execute() (line 47-52) iterates agents and returns '{"agent_id": "Result from agent_id"}' without calling any real task execution
AgentProxy.send_task() (line 19-20) returns 'f"Result from {self.name}"' unconditionally
The test test_swarm_execution asserts 'isinstance(results["A1"], str) and results["A1"]' which trivially passes against this stub
findings.suggestion: If the legacy shim is kept, document clearly that execute() returns placeholder strings and mark the tests as documentation of stub behavior with an explicit xfail.

findings.summary: trust_gateway.verify_capabilities() imports and accesses _DISCOVERY_ENGINE, a private attribute of mcp_bridge
evidence: Lines 530-554: 'from codomyrmex.agents.pai.mcp_bridge import _DISCOVERY_ENGINE' with a comment 'mcp_bridge._DISCOVERY_ENGINE is private but we can access it for now'
The code comment on line 530 explicitly acknowledges this is not the intended interface — a documented violation of encapsulation
findings.suggestion: Add a public get_discovery_metrics() function to mcp_bridge.py that returns the relevant metrics, removing the need for trust_gateway to access a private attribute.

findings.summary: trusted_call_tool appears twice in __all__ of trust_gateway.py
evidence: Line 821 and line 823 both contain '"trusted_call_tool"' in the __all__ list
Duplicate __all__ entries indicate the list was assembled without review — Python does not raise an error for duplicates so the bug is silent

findings.summary: performance/__init__.py has an import statement after __all__, creating confusing load order
evidence: Line 105: 'from . import analysis, benchmarking  # noqa: E402, F401' appears after __all__ is fully defined (lines 88-103)
The noqa comment acknowledges it violates E402 — the violation was suppressed rather than fixed
These submodule imports are side-effect imports placed after __all__ rather than at the top with the other imports, making the module's initialization contract non-obvious
findings.suggestion: Move the analysis and benchmarking imports to the top of the file with the other imports, before __all__, and remove the noqa suppression.

findings.summary: check_doc_links.py has its module docstring placed after import statements, not at file top
evidence: Lines 1-6 are import statements (import os, re, sys, Path, get_logger)
The module docstring 'Documentation Link Validation Script...' begins at line 8, after the imports
Python module docstrings must be the first statement in a file to be recognized by the __doc__ attribute — placing them after imports breaks this convention
findings.suggestion: Move the module docstring to line 1, before all import statements.

findings.summary: test_swarm_execution validates that strings are non-empty but the implementation returns hardcoded fake strings
evidence: test_swarm_execution asserts 'isinstance(results["A1"], str) and results["A1"]' — trivially true for any non-empty string
protocols/swarm.py line 51 returns 'f"Result from {agent.agent_id}"' unconditionally — this is a hardcoded stub
The test passes without verifying any task routing, execution, or result mapping logic — it tests the shape of hardcoded data, not behavior
findings.suggestion: Either replace the legacy shim with real delegation to the async SwarmManager, or mark these tests with an explicit xfail explaining that they document stub behavior.

findings.summary: Top-level scripts/ directory (105 entries) mirrors src/codomyrmex/ module structure without clear boundary
evidence: The scripts/ directory contains 105 entries organized as scripts/cloud, scripts/agents, scripts/audio, etc., mirroring the src/codomyrmex module layout
scripts/cloud/examples/basic_usage.py manually patches sys.path to import from src/ when the package is not installed
This creates parallel namespaces for 'cloud', 'agents', etc. with no documented rule for when something belongs in scripts/ vs src/codomyrmex/
findings.suggestion: Define an explicit policy: scripts/ contains standalone entry points that must not duplicate module logic. Examples and demos should live in src/codomyrmex/<module>/examples/ or be invoked via the CLI.

findings.summary: MCP auto-discovery scans all mcp_tools.py files with no per-module opt-in, creating implicit coupling at the system boundary
evidence: The PAI bridge uses pkgutil to scan every mcp_tools.py in the src/codomyrmex package tree and surfaces any @mcp_tool function as an MCP tool with no allowlist or per-module registry
CLAUDE.md documents 39 auto-discovered modules but does not describe how a module can prevent its tools from being surfaced
Any new mcp_tools.py file immediately becomes part of the public MCP surface with no deliberate registration step, creating implicit architectural coupling
findings.suggestion: Add an explicit __mcp_exported__ = True flag in each module's __init__.py or maintain a central allowlist in mcp_bridge.py, so module authors explicitly declare intent to export tools rather than relying on file presence.

findings.summary: Auto-injected YAML config loading blocks inside run_swarm() and harvest() load config_data that is never used
evidence: mega_swarm_dispatcher.py lines 101-109: config loading block appears inside run_swarm() after the swarm has already been dispatched; config_data is assigned but never referenced
mega_swarm_harvester.py lines 74-82: identical config block inside harvest() after print('Successfully applied...'); config_data is unused
run_colony.py lines 15-22: config block is at the top of main() but config_data is never passed to Colony(), Population(), or any other call in the function
findings.suggestion: Remove the auto-injected config blocks entirely since config_data is unused in all three cases, or move config loading to module level and pass values as parameters to the functions that need them.

findings.summary: mega_swarm_dispatcher.py converts sys.argv[1] to int with no ValueError guard and calls exit(1) inside a library function
evidence: Line 114: 'limit = int(sys.argv[1]) if len(sys.argv) > 1 else None' checks argument presence but not integer validity — './mega_swarm_dispatcher.py foo' raises an unhandled ValueError traceback
Line 67 inside dispatch_jules() calls 'exit(1)' directly — mixing process termination into a function makes it untestable without subprocess isolation and prevents the caller from handling the error
findings.suggestion: Wrap the int() conversion in try/except ValueError and print a usage message. Replace exit(1) in dispatch_jules() with 'raise RuntimeError("jules CLI not found")' so the caller controls termination.
